coredata.dc:title: Blue and green luminescence of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Štengl, Václav
Bakardjieva, Snejana
Henych, Jiří
Lang, Kamil
Kormunda, Martin
coredata.dc:description: We report on a new method for synthesising strongly blue and green photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Graphene was prepared by a new feasible method using an intensive cavitation field in a pressurised ultrasonic batch reactor. The prepared graphene was quantitatively converted to graphene oxide using our modified, safer Hummer’s method. Graphene oxide was characterised by microscopic (AFM and TEM) and spectral (infrared and Raman) methods, and the thermal stability of graphene oxide was determined using thermal analysis (DTA-TG). GQDs were prepared by a one-pot reaction, refluxing graphene oxide in different solvents (ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol, dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) at atmospheric pressure. The synthesised GQDs were characterised by infrared, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and AFM microscopy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DTA-TG of the graphene oxide prepared by the oxidation of graphene.

Photographs of blue (a) and green photoluminescent GQDs (b) prepared by refluxing GO in ethylene glycol (EG_GQDs) and dimethylformamide (DMF_GQDs) and excited at 365nm (right images). The red tracks in left images are along a laser beam.
AFM images of GQDs obtained in EG.
AFM images of GQDs obtained in DMF.
AFM images of GQDs obtained in DMSO.
Normalized luminescence spectra of GQDs with varying excitation wavelengths specified on the right side. GQDs were prepared in (A) ETG, (B) DMF, (C) DMSO, and (d) N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone.
High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s peak acquired on (top) dried Si wafer, (middle) residua of DMSO_GQDs and (bottom) residua of NMP_GQDs.
Compositions of the samples NMP_GQDs and DMSO_GQDs.

head.ce:title: Blue and green luminescence of reduced graphene oxide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Solid State Chemistry, Institute of Inorganic Chemistry AS CR v.v.i., 250 68 Řež, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Inorganic Chemistry AS CR v.v.i.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, J.E. Purkyně University in Ústí nad Labem, 400 96 Ustí nad Labem, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: J.E. Purkyně University in Ústí nad Labem

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on a new method for synthesising strongly blue and green photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Graphene was prepared by a new feasible method using an intensive cavitation field in a pressurised ultrasonic batch reactor. The prepared graphene was quantitatively converted to graphene oxide using our modified, safer Hummer’s method. Graphene oxide was characterised by microscopic (AFM and TEM) and spectral (infrared and Raman) methods, and the thermal stability of graphene oxide was determined using thermal analysis (DTA-TG). GQDs were prepared by a one-pot reaction, refluxing graphene oxide in different solvents (ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol, dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) at atmospheric pressure. The synthesised GQDs were characterised by infrared, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and AFM microscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) have applications in electronics for the development of light-emitting diodes, single-photon detectors, and lasers for optical fibre communication, optical memories, and laser printers and pointers. In addition, QDs are essential for medical diagnostics and non-invasive and invasive therapies of all kinds, as well as for diverse military and security uses, such as weapon systems, range finders, infrared sights, and security systems.

CdSe [1] and CdSe/ZnS [2] or CdTe [3] and CdTe/CdS [4] nanocrystals are widely used in biological applications, and GaAs [5] and InAs [6] are used in electronic applications. Their main disadvantage is the incorporation of toxic elements (Cd, As), which may be released into the environment. Therefore, Q-Dots are currently being developed from less toxic materials such as GaN [7], InP [8] and ZnS [9]. Q-Dots are most often prepared in non-aqueous solvents. This type of synthesis is quite challenging because it requires processing at high temperatures and usually involves the use of volatile and toxic chemical precursors (e.g., dimethylcadmium).

Alternatively, Q-Dots can be prepared in an aqueous environment. However, this synthesis method involves toxic gases such as H2S, H2Te, or H2Se. Both the aqueous and non-aqueous approaches are based on the creation of nanocrystals in the presence of stabilising ligands, which ensures their solvation in the reaction environment. The organic solvents most frequently used to stabilise the mixture are trioctylphosphine/trioctylphosphine oxide (TOP/TOPO). In water, various thiols are used (such as sulfanyl ethane acid). Q-Dots are then formed by mixing with suitable precursors and by subsequent heating.

Q-Dots based on graphene (GQDs) are mainly characterised by their non-toxicity, which determines their use in many applications, mostly in medicine and biology. Another advantage is their ease of preparation, without toxic precursors and with stabilisation in aqueous and non-aqueous media.

Small sheets of blue luminescent reduced graphene oxide with an average diameter of 30–50nm were prepared by using a simple and eco-friendly solvothermal process, by autoclaving in ethylene glycol under 180°C for 20h [10]. A facile hydrazine hydrate reduction of graphene oxide with surface passivation by polyethylene glycol was performed to fabricate graphene quantum dots [11]. Zhu et al. prepared GQDs with strongly blue and green fluorescence by the microwave pyrolysis of poly(ethylene glycol) and saccharide (glucose, fructose) [12] and by graphene oxide dispersion in dimethylformamide heated in a Teflon autoclave at 200°C for 5 to 8h [13–15].

Pan et al. [16] developed a hydrothermal route to cut graphene sheets into GQDs exhibiting blue luminescence. The product consisted of a yellow to brown transparent (colloid) suspension and black precipitate, which was removed. The yield of these reactions was about 1%. A new, one-step wet chemical process at atmospheric pressure turned carbon fibres into GQDs in the presence of sulphuric acid [17]. Surface functionalisation was found to be an effective method to tune the properties of graphene and graphene oxide (GO). Lu et al. [18] reported that GO hybrid materials with blue emission can be fabricated through GO surface functionalisation with aryl diazonium salts of 2-aminoanthracene. Fluorescent nitrogen-enriched carbon nanodots (C-dots) measuring 1–3nm were obtained through a one-pot reaction between melamine and glycerol. These C-dots show quantum yields of up to 22% and a high two-photon absorption cross-section [19]. Graphene quantum dots synthesised by a direct chemical method have been used in combination with ZnO nanowires to demonstrate their potential as a solar harvesting material in photovoltaic cells [20].

This study [21] reports on the synthesis of hydrophobic blue fluorescent material by covalent linking of octadecylamine to 5nm nanodiamond particles. Fluorescent nitrogen-enriched carbon nanodots of 1–3nm in size were obtained through a one-pot reaction between melamine and glycerol [19]. Ultrasonic preparation of GQDs and subsequent coupling with TiO2 for photocatalytic purposes was demonstrated Zhuo et al. [22]. Recent developments in preparation of GQDs and possible applications in bioimaging, electrochemical biosensors and catalysis, and in photovoltaic devices summarized Shin et al. [23]. Jiang [24] critically review the recent progress on the chemical preparation of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensors.

Fluorinated graphene quantum dots were synthesized by cutting fluorinated graphene through a hydrothermal approach with oxygen-rich functional groups with a F/C atomic ratio of ca. 23.68% and diameter of 1–7 [25]. Highly photoluminescent carbon dots with a PL quantum yield of 26% have been prepared in one step by hydrothermal treatment of orange juice [26]. In this review [27] are described the recent advances in the research on C-dots, focusing on their synthesis, surface functionalization, PL properties, and applications in photocatalysis, energy and sensor issues. This article [28] selectively reviews recent advances in the field of graphene-based materials for biosensing and bioimaging, the graphene-based enzyme biosensors, DNA biosensors, and immunosensors are summarized in detail.

Herein, we present a new, one-pot, scalable, and cost-effective method for GQD preparation from GO using organic solvents such as ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycols, dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone. The GQD thus formed are highly luminescent, with the emission maxima controlled by the excitation wavelength used.

All chemical reagents used in the present experiments were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. Ethylene glycol HO–CH2CH2–OH anhydrous 99.8%, poly-ethylene glycol (Mn=200 and 300) H(OCH2CH2) n OH, N,N-dimethylformamide HCON(CH3)2 anhydrous 99.8%, dimethyl sulfoxide and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone C5H9NO anhydrous 99.5% were supplied by Sigma–Aldrich.

Graphene was produced from natural graphite (Koh-i-noor Grafite Ltd., Czech Republic) using a high-intensity cavitation field in a pressurised batch ultrasonic reactor (UIP2000hd, 6bar, 20kHz, 2000W, Hielscher Ultrasonics GmbH). One gram of graphite was suspended in 140ml of ethylene glycol and sonicated for 20min. The final product was washed with water using dialysis (Spectra/Por 3 dialysis membrane) until the conductivity of distilled water was reached (∼5μS), filtered off and dried at 105°C [29].

Graphene oxide was prepared by improved Hummers method. Typically, 60ml of H2SO4 and 10ml of H3PO4, 1g of graphene and 3g of KMnO4 were mixed in a round-bottom flask. The mixture was then heated to 40°C and stirred for 6h and a pink dense suspension was obtained. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was poured onto ice in 200ml of 30% H2O2. The pink suspension quickly changed into a lemon-like yellow suspension. The product was purified by dialysis (Spectra/Por 3 dialysis membrane) and centrifuged. The final graphene oxide product was a brown suspension with the consistency of honey. When the starting material for oxidation by the modified Hummers method is raw graphite, the resulting product contains black unreacted agglomerates. In opposite, when it is used precursor prepared by sonication in our reactor, then is obtained only brown suspension freely dilutable to clear solution.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by refluxing 1ml of GO suspension in different organic solvents at atmospheric pressure (ethylene glycol, dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone) for 24h. The resulting solution was filtered off using a Pragopor membrane filter.

Blue and green photoluminescent GQDs were prepared by refluxing GO in ethylene glycol (EG_GQDs) and dimethylformamide (DMF_GQDs). The refluxing of GO with N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone gives yellow-green NMP_GQDs, while other solvents such as dimethyl sulfoxide, PEG 200 and PEG 300 react in the same way as ethylene glycol to give blue GQDs. Boiling in PEG with Mn greater than 300 did not provide luminescent GQDs by boiling at atmospheric pressure. Blank test heating of pure solvent at the boiling point without the presence of graphene oxide was performed and no luminescence was observed.

Diffraction patterns were collected using a PANalytical X́Pert PRO diffractometer equipped with a conventional X-ray tube (Cu Kα radiation, 40kV, 30mA, 1.5418nm) and a linear position sensitive detector PIXcel with an anti-scatter shield. A programmable divergence slit set to a fixed value of 0.5°, Soller slits set to a value of 0.02rad and a mask measuring 15mm in thickness were used in the primary beam. A programmable anti-scatter slit set to a fixed value of 0.5°, Soller slits set to a value of 0.02rad, and a Ni beta-filter were used in the diffracted beam. A qualitative analysis was performed with the DiffracPlus Eva software package (Bruker AXS, Germany) using the JCPDS PDF-2 database [30]. Samples of GQDs were dripped onto a dry silicon sample holder to form a thin transparent layer. The crystallite size, interlayer spacing and number of GQDs layers were calculated by using the classical Debye–Scherrer equations [31,32].

AFM images were obtained using an NTEGRA Aura (NT-MTD) microscope. A sample of the diluted dispersion was placed on synthetic mica as an atomically smooth support and evaporated at room temperature. The measurements were performed at room air temperature in non-contact mode with Si tips of the 1650–00 type at resonance frequencies ranging from 180 to 240kHz.

The morphology of the prepared graphene oxide was inspected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a 200kV TEM microscope JEOL 2010F. As a specimen support for TEM investigations, a microscopic copper grid covered by a thin transparent carbon film was used.

The particle size distribution was determined by laser scattering using a ZEN 1600 instrument (Malvern Co.). The sample was tested in a square quartz cuvette with a round aperture (PCS8501). UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 19 spectrophotometer. The steady-state luminescence properties of the samples were monitored using a Fluorolog 3 spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). The spectra were recorded with a cooled TBX-05-C photon detection module upon excitation at a given wavelength using a 450W Xe lamp.
Infrared spectra were recorded by using a Nicolet Impact 400D spectrometer over the range of approximately 4000–500cm−1 with an accessory ATR crystal (ZnSe, 500 scans, resolution 4cm−1).

DTA–TG–MS measurements were carried out by using a Netzsch Instrument STA 409 coupled to a Quadrupole Mass Spectrometer Balzers QMS-420 run simultaneously under dynamic conditions in air (flow rate 75mLmin− 1). The samples were heated at a rate of 10Kmin− 1.

The Raman spectra of the prepared graphene oxide were acquired with a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific) using a 532nm (0.3mW) laser; 1024 two-second scans were accumulated with the laser using the 10x objective of an Olympus microscope.

XPS apparatus was equipped with SPECS X-ray XR50 (Al cathode 1486.6eV) and SPECS PHOIBOS 100 Hemispheric Analyzer with 5-channels detector. A background pressure in XPS during the measurements was under 2×10−8 mbar. XPS survey-scan spectra were made at pass energy of 40eV; the energy resolution was set to 0.5eV. While individual high-resolution spectra were taken at pass energy of 10eV with 0.05eV energy steps. A software tool CasaXPS was used to fit high-resolution multi components peaks. The proper surface charge compensation was done by fitting C–C, C–H component of C 1s peak to reference binding energy 284.5eV. The atomic concentration of compounds was evaluated with relative sensitivity factors (RSF) defined in standard table of CasaXPS software. The drop of colloid solution of NMP_GQDs or DMSO_GQDs was placed on polished Si without an pretreatment. Then the sample was placed to vacuum chamber and pumped down for about 30min then the dry sample was moved to XPS machine. The colloid solution residuum was visible by naked eyes on Si after drying. The diffusion vacuum pump was used.

In this study, graphene was prepared using an intense cavitation field in an ultrasonic pressurised batch reactor [29]. Thus prepared graphene may be converted to graphene oxide with lower concentrations of strong acids and oxidising agents, lower reaction temperature and therefore under safer conditions than by conventional purely chemical synthesis routes. The milder conditions based on intense cavitation field would consequently allow the process scaling. Another advantage is that the oxidation can be fully quantitative.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to determine the quality of prepared graphene oxide (GO) used to produce GQDs. TEM images of the GO prepared by our modified oxidation method are presented in Supplement Fig. S1. As shown in the figure, the prepared GO formed large, smooth plates of ∼5×5μm in size. The AFM images (Supplement Fig. S2) show that, according to cross-sectional analysis, GO with a thickness of 0.87nm was prepared, which indicates that monolayer sheets of GO were successfully obtained [33]. Supplement Fig. S3 shows the Raman spectrum of GO. In this spectrum, the G band is broadened and shifted slightly to 1605cm−1, whereas the intensity of the D band at 1350cm−1 is substantially enhanced. The enhanced background of the spectrum is caused by sample fluorescence. The G band is common to all sp2 carbon forms and provides information on the in-plane vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms, and the D band indicates the presence of sp3 defects [34].

Supplement Fig. S4 shows the FTIR spectrum of prepared graphene oxide. The broad intense band at 3450cm−1 is assigned to O–H stretching vibrations of the C–OH groups, and the bands at 1740 and 1630cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching vibrations from carbonyl and carboxylic groups. The next peak at 1410cm−1 is attributed to skeletal vibrations from unoxidised graphitic domains. The peaks at 850 and 1200cm−1 represent the bending and asymmetric stretching modes of the epoxy C−O−C group, while the peak at 1050cm−1 is characteristic of alkoxy C−O vibrations [35].

The method with intense cavitation field has been shown to be a good alternative to oxidation processes using Hummer’s method [36], which needs large amount of aggressive chemical agents and generally have yields only about 20%, Anther synthetic route, the vapour deposition (CVD) of graphene provides perfectly flat layers of graphene, but it does not allow for the preparation of large batches of samples, which could be demanded for various applications. The preparation of graphene in an intense cavitation field has the following advantages:
•
it is possible to increase the scale of production without the formation of toxic gases and explosive intermediates because the method does not require the use of any acids or oxidising agents as in Hummer’s [36] method,
•
the reaction is quantitative under defined conditions (ultrasonic power density, time of reaction, weight of graphite),
•
the effects of a cavitation field generate uniform, spatially non-deformed particles due to the lack of chemical attack on graphite.

The decomposition temperature of GO prepared with Hummer’s method [36], as measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), was determined to be 200°C [13]. This temperature is of particular significance for the synthesis process because temperatures below 200°C cannot produce GQDs from graphene oxide effectively [13]. Based on the DTA-TG results of the graphene oxide prepared by the oxidation of graphene, decomposition occurs at a lower temperature; it starts at 97°C and ends at 183°C (see Fig. 1 ). For this reason, it is possible to prepare GQDs at atmospheric pressure under 24h of reflux at the boiling point of solvents, for example, in ethylene glycol at 197.3°C, in dimethylformamide at 154°C, in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone at 202°C and in dimethyl sulfoxide at 189°C.

During the reaction, GO is broken and reduced to nanoparticles of graphene. The surface of these sheets is passivated by the molecules of a proper solvent, which also acts as a weak reduction agent. In this study, large GO sheets were split and reduced simultaneously, and thus, GQDs were obtained. From a comparison of the oxygen content in the starting graphene oxide and the resulting product of quantum dots based on XPS analysis (see Table 1 ) is obvious that solvents have a reducing effect on the graphene oxide.

Photographs of blue and green photoluminescent GQDs prepared by refluxing GO in ethylene glycol (EG_GQDs) and dimethylformamide (DMF_GQDs), respectively, and excited at 365nm are presented in Fig. 2 a and b.
One of the advantages of this synthesis procedure is that the reaction is performed at atmospheric pressure, allowing scaling.

The infrared spectra of EG_GQDs and DMF_GQDs obtained after transferring the GQDs to diethyl ether (by shaking) and evaporating are presented in Supplement Fig. S5. The ATR FT-IR spectrum of the EG_GQDs (see Supplement Fig. S5a) shows characteristic vibrations, including a broad and intense O-H peak (3400cm−1), a C–OH stretching peak (1203cm−1), an C–O stretching peak (1025cm−1), and a weak peak assigned to O–H deformations of the C–OH groups (1396cm−1). The −CH and −CH2 stretching of alkanes is responsible for the vibrational bands at about 2935 and 2880cm−1 [39,40].

Supplement Fig. S5b shows a representative ATR infrared transmittance spectrum of as-prepared sample DMF_GQDs, with contributions from hydroxyls C–OH (1251cm−1), alkoxy vibrations C–O (1084cm−1), carboxyls COOH (1650cm−1), sp2-hybridised CC in-plane vibrations (1497cm−1), epoxides C–O–C (854cm−1) and amides C–N (1377cm−1) [41,42]. The emission wavelength of quantum-dot nanocrystals can be continuously tuned by changing the particle size, and a single light source can be used to simultaneously excite different-sized dots [43].

The luminescence properties of the GQD dispersions investigated at different excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 6 . The increase in the excitation wavelength led to a red shift in the luminescence spectra of the dispersions over emission wavelengths ranging from nearly 400–700nm. A comparison of the spectra shows that the resulting luminescence properties are affected by the refluxing solvent used during the synthesis. Thus, the excitation at 350nm leads to the luminescence bands with maxima at 430 (EG_GQDs), 477 (DMSO_GQDs), and 495nm (DMF_GQDs). The effect is less significant at longer excitation wavelengths; exciting at 500nm produces emission bands at 523 (EG_GQDs), 560 (DMSO_GQDs), 565nm (DMF_GQDs), and 570nm (NMP_GQDs). It has been shown that the luminescence properties of GQDs depend on particle size [44]. Small GQDs (1.2nm) are reported to exhibit UV emission (about 350nm), medium-sized GQDs (1.5–3nm) exhibit visible light emission (400–700nm), and large GQDs (3.8nm) emit in the near-infrared region (about 800nm) [45]. This excitation-dependent luminescence indicates that the polydispersity of the GQDs dispersions is affected by the method of synthesis. The emission maxima observed to vary between 410 and 610nm are typical for medium-sized GQDs and are in accordance with TEM and AFM measurements. The absorption bands of luminescent GQDs can be obtained by luminescence excitation spectroscopy (Supplement Fig. 6). The presence of particles with absorption bands at 340, 350 and 365nm and at approximately 425, 440 and 500nm is revealed.

The GQD prepared in MNP denoted as NMP_GQDs and in DMSO denoted as DMSO_GQDs were also analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to confirm that they are GQD. The XPS analyses of the dry residuum showed carbon, oxygen and silicon from underling substrate. There were also identified some traces of Na, S, N in sample DMSO_GQDs. The composition of Si wafer used as a support was also analysed before and after the drying procedure. The drying procedure increased the carbon concentration on the Si wafer from 5 to 12at.% measured in normal direction and from 13 to 16at.% when measured under angle of 60° when the analytical depth of XPS is limited. The two measurements were done on each GQD sample, a first measurement was in normal direction and the second measurement was done under angle of 60° from normal direction. It was expected to measure only the top surface of sample as shown on Si wafer above. But when the sample is not homogeneous flat film but the surface looks more like separated particles then the takeoff angle do not limit the depth of the measurements but the composition of the sides of the particles is measured because the measured photoelectrons leaves the surface perpendicularly. Therefore if the analysed surfaces are made from particles then the composition should be very similar under both angles and it was observed therefore the residuum is made of particles. The concentration of Si observed in normal direction can be higher than under angle of 60° due to a shadowing effect of particles on the uncovered space on Si wafer between them. It is supporting the idea the residuum is made from carbon particles. XPS spectra of C 1s peak on samples with residua of NMP_GQDs and DMSO_GQDs are presented in Fig. 7 together with C 1s peak from Si wafer after the drying procedure for comparison.

The high resolution carbon C 1s peak was fitted by a model of GQDs samples defined as follows; component C–C/CC (284.5eV – the calibration standard) and then oxygen bonded components, first at 286eV for C–O and second component at 288eV for CO bonds based on model in literature [46]. The abundances of components are concluded in Table 1. But the components C–C in C 1s peaks are influenced by the drying procedure as reported above on vacuum dried Si wafers. The additional carbon from the drying procedure is between 3 and 7at.% in total carbon content.

The C1s peak in carbon nanodots with nitrogen in the structure were in literature fitted by model with components 284.1, 284.5, 285.5 and 287.8eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N, C–O and CN respectively [47] it is practically identical model (apply+0.4eV charging correction) to our model. Similar nanodots were also investigated also by NMR where sp3 bonds were not found [27]. The literature reports also carbon nanodots where C 1s peak is composed only from CC and CO bonds [27]. The peak C 1s similar to sample NMP_GQDs were observed on carbon nanodots in literature [48]. Therefore there are more possibilities of C 1s structures for carbon nanodots.

The C 1s peak on samples with graphene oxide residua, prepared by the same procedure as carbon nanodots, were deconvoluted to components from another literature model [49] where the components C–C, C–OH, C–O–C and CO have binding energies about 284.5, 285.5, 286.6 and 288.1eV, respectively. Due to no nitrogen content in our samples the nitrogen related bonds in original model [49] were neglected.

The XPS model of carbon nanodots should be self-consisted and therefore the atomic concentrations of each bond should be in agreement in each element with respect to properties of XPS as for example different analytical depth for each electrons energy. The table shows at least some approximate qualitative agreement between content of oxygen and oxygen in bonds to carbon and silicon. The NMP_GQDs should have only small number on C–O bond due to presence of higher amount of Si–O in contrary to DMSO_GQDs where significant part of oxygen has to be bonded to carbon.

Because of their luminescence stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high water solubility, these GQDs are suitable as excellent probes for high-contrast bioimaging and biosensing applications. One major aspect to emphasise is the ease of preparation. Using our developed method for graphene synthesis, it is possible to prepare tens or hundreds of grams of high-quality product, which can be readily and safely converted to graphene oxide. Subsequent GQD synthesis proceeds at atmospheric pressure and at low temperatures, with a high yield of the desired product. These characteristics could allow for the industrial-scale production of stable, low-cost, non-toxic and highly effective luminescent agents based on graphene.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by RVO 61388980. The authors acknowledge the assistance of K. Šafářová (Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Faculty of Science, Palacký University in Olomouc) in conducting the AFM measurements, P. Bezdička and Z. Hájková (IIC) for XRD and Raman analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.07.031.
